summary: Before the war broke out, my family and I lived in North Hollywood, Los Angeles. I was born there in 1936, so I was about five or six when Pearl Harbor happened. It was a familiar place for us, but after the attack, life changed drastically and we eventually had to leave.

I remember after Pearl Harbor was attacked, there were blackouts and thick curtains over our windows to keep the light in. That event changed everything for us, and soon after, my father decided to move us inland to Utah because we were no longer considered safe in California. It was a frightening time for our family.

We moved to Utah after Pearl Harbor because it was far enough inland that we weren't seen as a threat. My father farmed sugar beets there, which was considered a war staple, so he was exempt from the draft. Life was hard; we weren't welcomed by the locals, and I remember the hunting license signs that included Japanese people, which was hurtful. We lived on farmland near Salt Lake and even saw German prisoners of war working on the farm.

While we were in Utah, I remember some Japanese families were already there, and the only name I recall is Endo. Though I don't remember the details, the Endo case was significant enough that it stuck in my mind. It was part of the larger context of Japanese American experiences during the war.

My father raised sugar beets in Utah during the war, which was considered a war staple crop. Because of this, he was exempted from the military draft. I remember the harvest time vividly, especially when German prisoners of war were brought to the farm to help with the work.

During the sugar beet harvest in Utah, German prisoners of war were brought to our farm to help with the labor. I had never seen such tall, pale people before, and I remember my father telling my mother to make coffee for them, which they appreciated. They were guarded and brought in by truck, and their lunches were simple, like thick bread with a thin slice of bologna.

From our kitchen window in Utah, we could see the Salt Lake and an irrigation channel that ran by our house. Living there was tough, especially because of the mosquitoes near the water. It was a stark contrast from our previous life in California.

My maternal grandparents moved further inland to Denver, Colorado during the war. We were lucky to visit them at least once while we were in Utah. I remember a difficult trip where my younger brother fell out of the car and got hurt, and it was hard to find a doctor willing to treat him because of the discrimination at the time.
